purpose: A comprehensive library of 26 Claude Code skill files covering the full open-webui development lifecycle — feature scaffolding, debugging, reference guides, and release workflows. Each skill encodes deep codebase-specific knowledge so Claude can execute recurring engineering tasks correctly the first time, without re-deriving conventions from scratch.

architecture.overview: Skills are organized as one directory per skill, each containing a single SKILL.md file. They fall into four functional tiers: (1) Feature Implementation — step-by-step scaffolding guides for adding backend or frontend artifacts; (2) Frontend UI — components, modals, workspace pages, and admin settings pages; (3) Debugging — systematic runbooks for diagnosing specific subsystems; (4) Reference — deep-dive documentation on cross-cutting systems (plugins, access control, config, DAG, migrations). The directory is flat — no nesting beyond the single skill-name/SKILL.md level. Skill names follow strict kebab-case and encode intent via a verb prefix (add-, debug-, cut-, pre-) or a noun suffix (-reference, -checklist).

subdirectories.purpose: Scaffold FastAPI CRUD endpoints with auth, error handling, and AccessGrants checks
Create Alembic migrations using the batch_alter_table pattern required for SQLite
Scaffold full database entities: SQLAlchemy model, Pydantic schemas, Form model, Repository class, and module-level singleton
Add hot-reloadable admin settings through the PersistentConfig system end-to-end
Author Pipe, Filter, Action, or Manifold plugins with correct Valves and dunder-parameter usage
Create Svelte UI components with store reactivity, i18n context, and onDestroy cleanup
Add frontend API client functions using the deferred-throw error pattern
Integrate new vector DB adapters by subclassing VectorDBBase
Add web search engine adapters with domain filtering support
Integrate new LLM providers with format conversion and streaming support
Create modal dialogs using the Modal.svelte shell with focus-trap and portal rendering
Add new workspace resource types (list/create/edit/delete) with a 6-file checklist
Scaffold admin settings pages with parallel loading and null-sentinel state initialization
Add LLM-callable built-in tools registered in get_builtin_tools()
Write Cypress E2E tests using aria-label selectors and tiered timeout constants
Run format, lint, type-check, and i18n checks before opening a PR to the dev branch
Full release workflow: version bump, changelog, push to main, monitor CI
Systematic runbook for debugging RAG, tools, filters, streaming, and routing in middleware.py
Debug vector search, collection naming, and embedding model mismatches
Debug Socket.IO delivery, session pool, and multi-instance Redis pub/sub issues
Debug Markdown rendering, message history DAG traversal, and streaming throttle issues
Reference for all 4 plugin types, Valves, dunder parameters, and SAFE_MODE behavior
Reference for the 3-layer access model: feature gates, admin bypass, per-resource grants
Reference for the PersistentConfig hot-reload system and correct read/write patterns
Reference for the message history DAG structure, branching, and correct traversal via buildMessages()
Reference for the dual migration systems (Peewee legacy + Alembic current) and batch_alter_table rules

architecture.data_flow: Skills are invoked by Claude Code in response to user task descriptions that match the trigger phrases in each skill's frontmatter description. The skill body then directs Claude through a deterministic sequence: read specific reference files → apply a required code template or pattern → run a concrete verification command. No runtime data flows between skills; each is self-contained. Reference skills (e.g., plugin-authoring-reference) are designed to be consulted by other skills or standalone, acting as lookup tables rather than procedural guides.

key_files.purpose: Most comprehensive scaffolding skill — covers all 5 layers of a new DB entity (SQLAlchemy model, Pydantic response/update schemas, Form model, Repository class with CRUD, module singleton). Establishes the canonical uuid/timestamp/JSONField conventions used across all backend skills.

Defines the required error-handling order (re-raise HTTPException before bare except), use of ERROR_MESSAGES enum, Depends(get_session), and the 3-layer access check sequence. Every backend endpoint must follow this pattern.

Encodes the Alembic batch_alter_table pattern mandatory for SQLite ALTER TABLE compatibility. Shows exactly how to generate, review, and squash migrations without breaking the dual Peewee/Alembic migration history.

Authoritative reference for all 4 plugin types (Pipe, Filter, Action, Manifold), Valves as the only persistent config mechanism, dunder parameter injection/stripping, and SAFE_MODE behavior. Required reading before touching any plugin code.

Canonical frontend skill — covers store spread-reassign reactivity pattern, i18n getContext (never import), onDestroy cleanup, and the deferred-throw API client error pattern. The pattern here applies to all Svelte work.
Documents the 3-layer access control model and the critical gotcha that BYPASS_ADMIN_ACCESS_CONTROL defaults to TRUE. Essential for any endpoint or resource that enforces permissions.

Documents PersistentConfig hot-reload behavior, correct .value read access, and the fact that assignment auto-persists to DB and syncs via Redis. Prevents the common mistake of reading config via os.environ directly in routers.

Documents the message history DAG structure (branching, currentId pointer) and mandates using buildMessages(history, currentId) — never iterating history directly. Critical for any frontend feature touching chat messages.
Step-by-step runbook for debugging the most complex subsystem — the middleware.py chat pipeline. Covers RAG injection, tool routing, filter chain, streaming, and model routing in order.
Shows the full end-to-end path for a new config setting: env.py → config.py → main.py app.state → router Depends → frontend API → admin settings UI. Any shortcut in this chain breaks hot-reload.
Reference for the dual migration systems — explains why both Peewee (legacy) and Alembic (current) coexist, which tables each owns, and the rule to never edit existing migration files.
Actionable pre-PR gate: exact commands for format, lint, type-check, and i18n validation. Defines what 'done' means before opening any PR to dev.

patterns.name: Frontmatter-gated skill trigger
patterns.description: Every SKILL.md begins with YAML frontmatter containing `name` (kebab-case, matches directory name) and `description` (one paragraph that includes explicit trigger phrases AND a negative scope clause, e.g. 'Use this when adding a new API endpoint. Do NOT use for modifying existing endpoints.'). This allows Claude Code to match the skill to a user request without hallucinating scope. Angle brackets are forbidden in frontmatter values.

patterns.name: 5-section body structure
patterns.description: Feature implementation skills follow a consistent 5-section body: (1) Overview / When to use — 2-3 sentence scoping statement. (2) Reference files — 3+ real file paths to read before starting. (3) Step-by-step instructions — numbered, concrete, with inline code examples using actual codebase identifiers. (4) Gotchas / Anti-patterns — a dedicated section of things NOT to do, each paired with the correct alternative. (5) Verification — a concrete command or checklist to confirm the implementation is correct (e.g., `alembic upgrade head`, `npm run check`, `pytest tests/test_entity.py`).

patterns.name: 3+ codebase-specific file references
patterns.description: Every skill (implementation and reference alike) cites at minimum 3 real file paths from the open-webui repo. These serve two purposes: they anchor Claude to the actual code rather than generic patterns, and they act as implicit read instructions — Claude will read these files before generating code. Paths always use the project-relative form (e.g., `backend/open_webui/routers/chats.py`, not a hypothetical path).

patterns.name: Deferred-throw API client error pattern
patterns.description: All frontend API client functions use a specific error pattern: the function catches errors and returns null, but also stores the error for the caller to throw. Concretely: `return fetch(...).then(r => r.json()).catch(err => { error = err.detail; return null; })`. Callers then check for null and rethrow. This is mandated in add-api-client-function and referenced in add-svelte-component — it is the only acceptable frontend error pattern.

patterns.name: HTTPException re-raise ordering
patterns.description: Backend error handlers must always re-raise HTTPException BEFORE catching bare `except Exception`. The pattern is: `try: ... except HTTPException: raise except Exception as e: raise HTTPException(...)`. Reversing this order causes HTTPException to be swallowed and return 500 instead of the intended status code. This pattern appears in every router skill and the debug-chat-pipeline runbook.

patterns.name: Svelte store spread-reassign reactivity
patterns.description: Svelte store updates must use spread-then-reassign to trigger reactivity: `store = { ...store, key: value }` for objects, `items = [...items, newItem]` for arrays. Direct mutation (`store.key = value`) does not trigger reactive updates. This convention is consistently enforced across all frontend skills.

patterns.name: Tiered debug runbook format
patterns.description: Debug skills follow a tiered structure that mirrors the actual execution order of the subsystem being debugged. Each tier is: (1) What to look for, (2) Where in the code to look (specific file + function), (3) What log output or assertion confirms the issue. The tiers progress from outermost (e.g., HTTP layer) to innermost (e.g., embedding model), so developers can stop as soon as they find the fault without reading the whole document.

patterns.name: Reference skill as lookup table
patterns.description: Reference skills (suffixed -reference) are structured as lookup tables, not procedures. They use definition lists or two-column tables: left column is the concept or parameter name, right column is the exact behavior or value. They always include a 'Common mistakes' section at the end. They are designed to be consulted by other skills (via Claude's context) and by developers directly.

conventions.naming: Directory names are kebab-case verb-noun pairs (add-entity-model, debug-chat-pipeline, cut-release). The verb encodes intent: 'add-' for feature scaffolding, 'debug-' for diagnostic runbooks, 'pre-' for gates/checklists, 'cut-' for release operations, noun-only with '-reference' suffix for lookup tables. The directory name must exactly match the `name` field in the SKILL.md frontmatter. All files are named SKILL.md (uppercase, no variation).
conventions.imports: Skills reference files using project-relative paths from the open-webui repo root (e.g., backend/open_webui/routers/chats.py, src/lib/apis/chats/index.ts). No absolute paths. No hypothetical or example paths — every cited file must actually exist in the repo.
conventions.error_handling: Two tiers: backend uses HTTPException re-raise-first ordering with ERROR_MESSAGES enum (never inline strings); frontend uses the deferred-throw pattern where API functions return null on error and store the error detail for the caller to rethrow. Both patterns are mandated to be consistent — mixing them is explicitly called out as an anti-pattern in multiple skills.
other: Skill body length: implementation skills target 150-300 lines; reference skills can be longer; checklist/debug skills are typically 80-150 lines.

Code examples use real identifiers from the open-webui codebase — no placeholder names like 'MyModel' or 'yourFunction'. Examples reference actual files like `Chats`, `Messages`, `Users`, `middleware.py`.
Every prohibition ('never do X') is paired with its alternative ('do Y instead') — standalone prohibitions without alternatives are not allowed.
i18n strings: always retrieved via `getContext('i18n')` in Svelte components, never imported as a module. This is called out as a hard rule in every frontend skill.
UUID generation: always `str(uuid.uuid4())` — never `uuid.uuid4()` alone (would be a UUID object, not a string, breaking JSON serialization).
Timestamps: always `int(time.time())` stored as BigInteger — never datetime objects in the DB layer.
Skills are not versioned individually — the entire skills directory evolves with the codebase.

internal: test-repos/backend/open_webui/routers/ — all add-router-endpoint, add-entity-model, add-config-setting skills reference files here
test-repos/backend/open_webui/models/ — entity model and migration skills reference this layer
test-repos/backend/open_webui/middleware.py — debug-chat-pipeline is entirely organized around this file
test-repos/src/lib/apis/ — all frontend API client skills reference this directory structure
test-repos/src/lib/stores/index.ts — all frontend component skills note that all 70+ stores are defined here
test-repos/src/lib/components/ — Svelte component skills reference Modal.svelte, specific UI primitives

external_key.name: Alembic
external_key.purpose: Database migration generation and application — add-db-migration and database-migration-reference are entirely about Alembic usage patterns

external_key.name: SQLAlchemy
external_key.purpose: ORM layer for all entity models — add-entity-model mandates specific SQLAlchemy column types (JSONField, BigInteger)

external_key.name: FastAPI
external_key.purpose: Backend router framework — add-router-endpoint covers Depends injection, HTTPException, and router registration

external_key.name: Svelte
external_key.purpose: Frontend framework — add-svelte-component, add-svelte-modal, add-admin-settings-page, add-workspace-resource all target Svelte-specific reactivity and lifecycle patterns

external_key.name: Socket.IO
external_key.purpose: Real-time event delivery — debug-socket-events covers session pool, multi-instance pub/sub, and delivery debugging

external_key.name: Cypress
external_key.purpose: E2E test framework — add-cypress-e2e-spec covers aria-label selectors, tiered timeouts, and test file placement

external_key.name: Redis
external_key.purpose: Config sync across instances — persistent-config-reference documents Redis as the pub/sub mechanism for hot-reload propagation

domain_terms.meaning: A class-level configuration object inside a plugin. The only reliable persistent state mechanism for plugins — instance variables are reset on each invocation.
Special injected parameters in plugin methods (e.g., __user__, __event_emitter__, __request__). They are stripped from the LLM tool spec automatically and must not be declared in the Valves schema.
A plugin type that exposes multiple virtual model IDs. IDs use dot notation and are split on the FIRST dot only to determine the manifold prefix.
The hot-reloadable configuration system. Values are stored in the DB, synced via Redis, and read via .value attribute. Assignment auto-persists.
Alembic context manager required for any SQLite ALTER TABLE operation. SQLite does not support ALTER COLUMN natively, so Alembic recreates the table inside this context.
The per-resource permission model (3rd layer). Checks whether a specific user has read/write/admin access to a specific resource instance.
An enum of all backend error strings. All HTTPException detail values must use this enum — inline strings are forbidden.
The only correct way to traverse the message history DAG. Never iterate history directly — the DAG has branches, and direct iteration produces incorrect orderings.
A plugin Filter subtype that runs on the response path. Does NOT run for direct /v1/chat/completions API requests — only for UI-originated requests.
Environment flag that disables all dynamic plugin execution. When SAFE_MODE=true, no Pipe/Filter/Action/Manifold plugins run, regardless of configuration.
Config flag that defaults to TRUE, meaning admins bypass all per-resource access checks by default. Developers frequently assume this defaults to false.
The correct SQLAlchemy column type for JSON data in open-webui. Column(JSON) is explicitly forbidden — it breaks cross-DB compatibility.
A chat model ID prefix that intentionally bypasses ownership checks. Not a bug — it is used for local model routing.
A user-facing entity accessible from the Workspace sidebar (e.g., knowledge bases, prompts, models). Adding one requires changes to 6 specific files as documented in add-workspace-resource.

gotchas.issue: BYPASS_ADMIN_ACCESS_CONTROL defaults to TRUE, not false. Admins bypass all per-resource AccessGrants checks unless this is explicitly disabled. Security-sensitive resources that should restrict admins require explicitly setting this to false.
gotchas.solution: Always check persistent-config-reference and access-control-reference before implementing any access-controlled resource. Never assume the default is restrictive.

gotchas.issue: HTTPException swallowing: placing `except Exception` before `except HTTPException` causes intentional 401/403/404 responses to be caught and converted to 500.
gotchas.solution: Always order: `except HTTPException: raise` first, then `except Exception as e: raise HTTPException(500, ...)`.

gotchas.issue: Setting Content-Type: multipart/form-data manually on FormData uploads breaks the multipart boundary. The browser must generate this header automatically.
gotchas.solution: Never set Content-Type when sending FormData. Let the fetch API omit it and the browser will append the correct boundary.

gotchas.issue: aiohttp is pinned at 3.13.3. Upgrading breaks streaming behavior in ways that are not immediately obvious in tests.
gotchas.solution: Never upgrade aiohttp. If a dependency requires a newer version, the conflict must be resolved at the dependency level, not by upgrading aiohttp.

gotchas.issue: Chat messages exist in two places: the `chat.chat` JSON column AND the normalized `chat_message` table. Reading one and ignoring the other produces stale or incomplete data.
gotchas.solution: Always use `Chats` model methods for reading/writing chat data — never query chat_message directly or parse chat.chat manually.

gotchas.issue: Plugin instance variables (non-Valve) are reset on every invocation. Storing state in `self.my_var = ...` in a plugin method loses the value on the next call.
gotchas.solution: All persistent plugin state must live in Valves. Use class-level Valve definitions, not instance variables.

gotchas.issue: Manifold plugin IDs use dot notation (e.g., myprovider.gpt-4) but are split on the FIRST dot only to find the manifold prefix. A model ID with multiple dots still resolves to the first-dot prefix.
gotchas.solution: Design Manifold IDs with the prefix before the first dot. Never rely on splitting on the last dot.

gotchas.issue: i18n in Svelte components must come from `getContext('i18n')`, not an import. Importing the i18n module directly breaks SSR and context propagation.
gotchas.solution: Always: `const i18n = getContext('i18n')` inside the component script. Never: `import i18n from '$lib/i18n'`.

gotchas.issue: Direct Svelte store mutation (`store.key = value`) does not trigger reactive updates — Svelte's reactivity requires assignment to the store reference itself.
gotchas.solution: Always use spread-reassign: `store = { ...store, key: value }` for objects; `items = [...items, newItem]` for arrays.

gotchas.issue: Outlet filters do not run for direct /v1/chat/completions API requests. Developers expecting filter logic to apply to API clients are surprised when it doesn't.
gotchas.solution: Outlet filters only fire for UI-originated requests. For API-accessible filtering, implement at the router level, not via plugins.

gotchas.issue: Nginx SSE buffering: Server-Sent Events are buffered by default in Nginx, causing streaming responses to appear to hang until the buffer fills.
gotchas.solution: Add `proxy_buffering off` to the Nginx location block for any streaming endpoint.

gotchas.issue: console.log is stripped in production builds. Debug logging via console.log only works in development — log statements will silently disappear in production.
gotchas.solution: For production-visible logging, use the backend logging infrastructure, not frontend console.log.

gotchas.issue: UUID stored as UUID object (not string) breaks JSON serialization. `str(uuid.uuid4())` produces a string; `uuid.uuid4()` alone produces a UUID object.
gotchas.solution: Always: `id = str(uuid.uuid4())`. The str() wrapper is mandatory in all entity models.

commands.purpose: Generate a new Alembic migration after modifying SQLAlchemy models
Apply all pending migrations — used as the verification step in add-db-migration

commands.command: npm run format && npm run lint && npm run check
commands.purpose: Pre-PR gate: format, lint, and TypeScript type-check the frontend (from pre-pr-checklist)
Verify a new entity model and repository after scaffolding
Run a specific Cypress E2E spec after authoring
Verify all i18n keys are present and no untranslated strings were added (required before PR)

skill_opportunities.name: add-redis-cache-layer
skill_opportunities.description: No skill exists for adding Redis caching to an existing endpoint or service. Given that Redis is already used for config sync, a skill for adding cache-aside patterns with TTL and invalidation would complement the existing infrastructure skills.
workflow_steps: Read backend/open_webui/utils/redis.py for existing Redis client usage
Add cache key namespace following existing conventions
Wrap the data-fetch call with cache-aside logic
Add cache invalidation on write operations
Verify with a test that bypasses cache via forced cache miss

skill_opportunities.name: add-websocket-handler
skill_opportunities.description: debug-socket-events documents Socket.IO debugging but there is no 'add' skill for creating new socket event handlers. WebSocket features are increasingly common in open-webui.
workflow_steps: Read backend/open_webui/socket/ for existing event registration patterns
Register new event on the correct namespace
Add session-aware handler with proper authentication check
Add corresponding frontend socket listener with cleanup in onDestroy
Verify event delivery in single-instance and multi-instance (Redis pub/sub) modes

skill_opportunities.name: add-background-task
skill_opportunities.description: No skill covers adding FastAPI BackgroundTasks or scheduled periodic tasks. Background processing is a recurring need for things like embedding jobs and cleanup tasks.
workflow_steps: Determine if the task is request-scoped (BackgroundTasks) or periodic (APScheduler/Celery)
Read existing background task registrations for pattern
Implement task function with proper error handling and logging
Register task at the correct lifecycle point
Verify task runs without blocking the request lifecycle
